perturbation.original_text: 1.5 Quality Control, Licensor Approvals. Licensor, as owner of the Licensed Mark, shall have the right at all times to control and approve the nature and quality of the Licensed Products (and the Licensed Mark thereon), and to inspect Licensee's business operations upon reasonable prior notice for the purpose of ensuring that a high level of quality of the Licensed Products is being maintained by Licensee.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 Quality Control, Licensor Approvals. Licensor, as owner of the Licensed Mark, will not have the right to control and approve the nature and quality of the Licensed Products (and the Licensed Mark thereon). Licensee is solely responsible for ensuring that quality standards are met, and Licensor's oversight is not required.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the original statement that the Licensor has the right to control and approve the quality of the Licensed Products. Now the licensee is the sole responsible to meet the qualities and the Licensor does not have oversight on quality. This contradicts the initial grant of authority and could lead to disputes over acceptable quality levels and liability for defects.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.5 Quality Control, Licensor Approvals. Licensor, as owner of the Licensed Mark, will not have the right to control and approve the nature and quality of the Licensed Products (and the Licensed Mark thereon). Licensee is solely responsible for ensuring that quality standards are met, and Licensor's oversight is not required. At Licensor's reasonable request during each calendar year, Licensee shall submit samples to Licensor, at no cost to Licensor, and shall not materially depart therefrom without Licensor's prior express written consent. The Licensed Products, as well as all promotional, packaging and advertising material relative thereto, shall include all appropriate legal notices as required by Licensor. No more frequently than once per year, a third party auditor chosen by Licensor and approved by Licensee, such approval not to be unreasonably withheld, shall be entitled at any time on reasonable notice to the Licensee to enter, during regular business hours, any premises used by the Licensee or its manufacturers for the manufacture, packaging or storage of the Licensed Products, to inspect such premises, all plant, workforce and machinery used for manufacture, packaging or storage of Licensed Products and all other aspects of the manufacture, packaging and storage of Licensed Products ("Access Rights"). Prior to exercising such Access Rights, the third party auditor shall enter into a nondisclosure agreement with Licensee that, among other terms deemed acceptable by Licensee and such third party auditor, shall: (a) limit the content of any report made by the third party auditor to Licensor to a description of the manner in which, and the conditions under which, the Licensed Mark are used by Licensee or its manufacturers; and (b) prevent the disclosure of any of Licensee's trade secrets and/or Confidential Information. To the extent reasonably practicable, all Licensed Products shall include notices on labeling and packaging for the Licensed Products stating that the Licensed Mark is owned by Licensor and used by Licensee under license from Licensor. The Licensed Products shall be of a quality commensurate with previous production or the samples approved by Licensor. If the quality of a class of the Licensed Products falls below such standards, Licensee shall use commercially reasonable efforts to restore such quality. In the event that Licensee has not taken appropriate steps to restore such quality within one-hundred twenty (120) days after notification by Licensor, Licensor shall have the right to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text granted the Licensor the right to control and approve the quality of the licensed products. The changed text explicitly denies this right, placing sole responsibility on the licensee. This creates a direct conflict with the remainder of section 1.5 (the Contradicted Text), which still contains provisions for the Licensor's involvement in quality control, such as submitting samples for approval, including legal notices required by Licensor, third-party audits chosen by Licensor, and the quality of the licensed products being commensurate with samples approved by Licensor. The changed text thus creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Termination for Breach. Licensor and Licensee will be entitled to terminate this Agreement by written notice to the other party in the event the other party is in material breach of any of its obligations hereunder and shall fail to remedy any such default within one hundred twenty (120) days after notice thereof by the non-breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Termination for Breach. Licensor will be entitled to terminate this Agreement by written notice to the Licensee in the event Licensee is in material breach of any of its obligations hereunder and shall fail to remedy any such default within one hundred twenty (120) days after notice thereof by the Licensor. However, the Licensee cannot terminate the agreement, even if the Licensor breaches the contract.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a significant in-text contradiction by stating that only Licensor can terminate the agreement for a breach, while the Licensee is barred from doing the same. This creates an imbalance of power and uncertainty about the Licensee's rights and remedies if the Licensor fails to meet its obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Termination for Breach. Licensor will be entitled to terminate this Agreement by written notice to the Licensee in the event Licensee is in material breach of any of its obligations hereunder and shall fail to remedy any such default within one hundred twenty (120) days after notice thereof by the Licensor. However, the Licensee cannot terminate the agreement, even if the Licensor breaches the contract.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that only the Licensor can terminate the agreement for breach, while the Licensee is barred from doing so, even if the Licensor breaches the contract. This directly contradicts the implied understanding (and often explicit right) in contract law that both parties generally have recourse in the event of a material breach by the other party. It creates a significant imbalance of power and uncertainty about the Licensee's rights and remedies if the Licensor fails to meet its obligations, thus creating an in-text contradiction, according to the provided 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction' definition. The contradicted text is the same location as the changed text; the addition of the last sentence of the changed text creates the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.10 Assignment. This Agreement may not be assigned by Licensee without the consent of Licensor which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.10 Assignment. This Agreement may not be assigned by Licensee, and the Licensor's consent can be withheld for any reason, including reasons that are unreasonable.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the original condition that Licensor's consent for assignment by Licensee shall not be unreasonably withheld. By removing this condition, the Licensor now has the power to withhold consent for any reason, reasonable or unreasonable. This limits Licensee's flexibility in the future and creates an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.10 Assignment. This Agreement may not be assigned by Licensee, and the Licensor's consent can be withheld for any reason, including reasons that are unreasonable. Notwithstanding the foregoing, no such consent of Licensor is required under this Agreement in the event of a Change of Control of Licensee so long as: (a) the resulting, surviving or transferee Person assumes all the obligations of the Licensee by operation of Law or pursuant to an agreement in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to the Licensor; and (b) the licenses granted herein shall not be transferrable or sublicensable to Affiliates of such Person unless such Affiliates were Affiliates of Licensee prior to such Change of Control.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies Section 6.10 by stating that the Licensor's consent for assignment by Licensee can be withheld for any reason, including unreasonable ones. This directly contradicts the original implication that consent could not be unreasonably withheld. Although it seems that both the changed text and contradicted text are the same. However, the change indicates that the original was intended to have a reasonable clause and this change creates an in-text contradiction as it alters the flexibility of the Licensee and it is an ambiguity as the key terms or obligations are defined inconsistently, specifically regarding the conditions under which consent for assignment can be withheld.
